skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Create AI avatar and talking head videos via inference.sh CLI. Generate AI presenters, UGC ads, explainer videos, and localized content from a single portrait image using P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse models.
skill.summary: Generate AI avatar and talking head videos with built-in TTS, multi-language support, and lipsync capabilities via a simple CLI workflow.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: The skill is a benign Markdown documentation file describing how to use the inference.sh 'belt' CLI for AI avatar video generation. All 56 static findings are false positives: the 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' alerts are inline code formatting in markdown (e.g., `belt app run ...`), and the 'Hardcoded URL' alerts are documentation links and example placeholder URLs in code samples. The skill uses `allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)` which is a strong security practice that restricts Bash execution to only the belt CLI command. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' finding at line 3 is a false positive — line 3 contains the skill description metadata, not any cryptographic code. The skill does not exfiltrate data, execute arbitrary code, or perform any malicious actions.

low_findings.title: Documentation references example URLs and CLI commands
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md file contains hardcoded URLs to documentation pages (inference.sh/docs) and example placeholder URLs (portrait.jpg, speech.mp3) within bash code samples. These are all part of legitimate documentation, not data exfiltration. The skill restricts bash execution to `Bash(belt *)` which is a strong security control.

content.user_title: Generate AI Avatar and Talking Head Videos
content.value_statement: Creating professional AI avatar videos typically requires expensive platforms and complex workflows. This skill enables you to generate talking head videos, UGC content, and multilingual presentations from a simple portrait image using the inference.sh CLI.
actual_capabilities: Generate AI avatar videos from a portrait image and text script using built-in TTS
Create talking head videos with multiple models including P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse
Produce multilingual avatar videos with 30 voices across 10 languages
Generate UGC-style content with custom voice tone and video prompt controls
Dub existing videos into other languages using transcription, translation, and lipsync workflows
Batch-generate multiple presenter variations from a single script
limitations: Requires inference.sh CLI (belt) to be installed and authenticated
Output quality depends on the input portrait image — front-facing photos with good lighting work best
Some models (OmniHuman, PixVerse) do not have built-in TTS and require separate audio generation
Video generation is API-dependent and incurs per-second processing costs

use_cases.title: Create UGC ads and product demos
use_cases.description: Generate authentic-looking UGC-style videos with AI presenters for marketing campaigns, product reviews, and social media ads.

use_cases.title: Produce educational explainer videos
use_cases.description: Create course content, training materials, and explainer videos with a consistent AI presenter across multiple lessons.

use_cases.title: Localize content into multiple languages
use_cases.description: Dub existing video content or generate new avatar videos in different languages using transcription, translation, and lipsync workflows.
use_cases.target_user: Localization teams and global content publishers

prompt_templates.title: Generate a quick avatar video with text script
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an AI avatar video for me. Use the P-Video-Avatar model. My portrait image is at [IMAGE_URL]. The script is: "Hello, welcome to our product demo!" Use a female voice and 720p resolution.

prompt_templates.title: Create UGC-style avatar with emotion control
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a UGC-style avatar video using P-Video-Avatar. Image: [IMAGE_URL]. Script: "Okay so I just tried this product and honestly? It is a game changer!" Voice: Zephyr (Female). Add voice prompt: excited, casual, authentic tone. Add video prompt: person talking casually to camera in a bright room with natural gestures. Resolution: 1080p.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate — marketing content with tone control

prompt_templates.title: Dub a video into another language
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to dub this video [VIDEO_URL] into Spanish. Step 1: transcribe the original audio. Step 2: translate the transcript to Spanish. Step 3: generate Spanish speech using Kokoro TTS. Step 4: use LatentSync to lipsync the original video with the new Spanish audio.

prompt_templates.title: Batch-generate multiple presenter variations
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate 3 avatar videos for the same product testimonial using different voices: Zephyr (Female), Puck (Male), and Aoede (Female). Image: [IMAGE_URL]. Script: "This changed my morning routine completely. Five minutes and I am done." Use 1080p resolution with a casual, authentic tone.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced — A/B testing and content scaling

output_examples.input: Portrait image + text script: 'Welcome to our product walkthrough. Today I will show you three key features.'
output_examples.output: A 720p talking head video with a natural-sounding AI voice reading the script, lip movements synchronized to the audio, produced in approximately 1.83 seconds of processing per second of video.
An audio-driven avatar video using OmniHuman 1.5, preserving all characters in the image with specified character animation matching the audio input.
A dubbed version of the original video with new audio in the target language, lipsynced using LatentSync to match the new speech while preserving the original video's visual content.

best_practices: Use front-facing, high-quality portrait photos with good lighting for best avatar generation results
Start with P-Video-Avatar for most use cases — it has built-in TTS and is 18x faster than alternatives
Generate input portrait images with 9:16 aspect ratio when creating vertical video content for social media
anti_patterns: Do not use P-Video-Avatar for multi-character images — use OmniHuman 1.5 instead for that scenario
Do not generate separate audio for models that have built-in TTS — it adds unnecessary cost and complexity
Do not use low-quality or side-profile photos as input — they produce poor avatar animations

faq.question: What is P-Video-Avatar and why is it recommended?
faq.answer: P-Video-Avatar is the recommended model because it is 18x faster, 6x cheaper than alternatives, supports 1080p resolution, and has built-in TTS with 30 voices across 10 languages.

faq.question: Do I need a GPU or special hardware to run this skill?
faq.answer: No. All video generation runs on inference.sh cloud infrastructure via the belt CLI. You only need the CLI installed and an authenticated account.

faq.question: How do I install the belt CLI?
faq.answer: Run 'npx skills add belt-sh/cli' or follow the installation instructions at the inference.sh documentation. Then authenticate with 'belt login'.

faq.question: Can I dub an existing video into another language?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the dubbing workflow: transcribe with Whisper, translate the text, generate new audio with Kokoro TTS, then use LatentSync to lipsync the original video with the new audio.

faq.question: What aspect ratios are supported?
faq.answer: The output aspect ratio matches your input image. Generate portrait images with 9:16 for vertical social media content, or 16:9 for landscape videos.

faq.question: Can I generate multiple videos with different presenters from one script?
faq.answer: Yes. Use a bash loop to iterate over different voice options while keeping the same script and image. This is useful for A/B testing and content scaling.
